content:
# Exodus 27 -- Disagreement Log

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Translators:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)

---

## Verse-Level Disagreements

### v. 1 -- Sentence Structure
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And you shall make the altar of acacia wood: five cubits the length..." |
| LOGOS | "And you shall make the altar of acacia wood: five cubits the length..." |
| PNEUMA | "Build the altar out of acacia wood -- five cubits long..." |
| **SOPHIA** | **Follows EMET/LOGOS** -- preserves the Hebrew clause structure with *ve-asita* ("and you shall make") rather than PNEUMA's imperative "Build." The *ve-* conjunction and imperfect verb form warrant the standard "And you shall make" rendering. |
| **Severity** | **Minor** -- stylistic restructuring, no meaning change. |

### v. 3 -- *le-dashno*: "for removing its ashes" vs. "to receive its ashes"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "for removing its ashes" |
| LOGOS | "to receive its ashes" |
| PNEUMA | "for removing ashes" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Follows EMET/PNEUMA** -- *le-dashno* is piel infinitive construct of *dashan* ("to remove fat-ashes"), indicating active removal, not passive reception. LOGOS's "to receive" softens the verbal force. |
| **Severity** | **Minor** -- both are defensible; the piel form slightly favors the active sense. |

### v. 3 -- PNEUMA's gloss "for catching the blood"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "its basins" |
| LOGOS | "its basins" |
| PNEUMA | "its basins for catching the blood" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Follows EMET/LOGOS** -- PNEUMA's addition "for catching the blood" is interpretively accurate (cf. Lev 1:5, 11) but not in the Hebrew text. The consensus omits the explanatory gloss. |
| **Severity** | **Minor** -- added clarification, not mistranslation. |

### v. 5 -- Verb for *ve-natattah*: "put" vs. "set"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "you shall put" |
| LOGOS | "you shall set" |
| PNEUMA | "Set" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Follows LOGOS** -- "set" captures the deliberate positioning of the grating better than "put," which is more generic. |
| **Severity** | **Trivial** -- synonym choice, no meaning change. |

### v. 8 -- Capitalization of divine subject: "he showed" vs. "He showed"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "he showed" |
| LOGOS | "He showed" |
| PNEUMA | "you were shown" (passive) |
| **SOPHIA** | **Follows LOGOS** -- capitalizes "He" to indicate the divine subject. PNEUMA's passive construction ("you were shown") avoids naming the subject; while the hophal could support a passive rendering, the hiphil *her'ah* with implied divine subject warrants the active form. |
| **Severity** | **Minor** -- theological convention rather than grammatical dispute. |

### vv. 9, 11, 12, 14, 15 -- *qela'im*: "hangings" vs. "curtains"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "hangings" |
| LOGOS | "hangings" |
| PNEUMA | "curtains" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Follows EMET/LOGOS** -- "hangings" for *qela'im* preserves the distinction from *yeri'ot* ("curtains") used for the tabernacle fabric in ch. 26. PNEUMA's "curtains" collapses a meaningful Hebrew lexical distinction. |
| **Severity** | **Moderate** -- affects the reader's ability to track two distinct Hebrew terms across the tabernacle instructions. |

### v. 10 -- *adneihem*: "sockets" vs. "bases"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "sockets" |
| LOGOS | "bases" |
| PNEUMA | "bases" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Follows EMET** -- consistent with the decision at 26:19. "Sockets" captures the mechanical function of these pedestals as receiving housings for tenons or posts. "Bases" is accurate but less specific. |
| **Severity** | **Minor** -- both are defensible; the consensus maintains internal consistency. |

### vv. 14-15 -- *katef*: "shoulder" vs. "side of the entrance"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "shoulder" |
| LOGOS | "shoulder" |
| PNEUMA | "side of the entrance" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Follows EMET/LOGOS** -- "shoulder" preserves the Hebrew architectural body-metaphor (*katef*, literally "shoulder"). PNEUMA's "side of the entrance" is a functional paraphrase that loses the metaphor. |
| **Severity** | **Minor** -- PNEUMA's rendering is accurate in meaning but sacrifices the Hebrew idiom. |

### v. 16 -- *masakh*: sentence structure
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And for the gate of the courtyard, a screen of twenty cubits..." |
| LOGOS | "And for the gate of the courtyard shall be a screen of twenty cubits..." |
| PNEUMA | "For the entrance of the courtyard, make a screen twenty cubits wide..." |
| **SOPHIA** | **Follows EMET** -- concise nominal clause structure. LOGOS adds "shall be" unnecessarily. PNEUMA substitutes "entrance" for "gate" (*sha'ar* = "gate," the more specific term). |
| **Severity** | **Trivial** -- stylistic preferences; all convey the same meaning. |

### v. 19 -- *yetedot*: "pegs" vs. "tent pegs"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "pegs" |
| LOGOS | "tent pegs" |
| PNEUMA | "tent pegs" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Follows EMET** -- "pegs" for *yetedot* is sufficient; the tabernacle context makes the function self-evident. "Tent pegs" adds a specifier not in the Hebrew. |
| **Severity** | **Trivial** -- clarifying addition, no meaning change. |

### v. 20 -- Word order of *shemen zayit zakh katit*
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "oil of olives, pure, beaten" |
| LOGOS | "pure oil of beaten olives" |
| PNEUMA | "pure oil of pressed olives" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Follows LOGOS** -- "pure oil of beaten olives" produces the most natural English while preserving all four elements. EMET's Hebrew-order rendering is awkward in English. PNEUMA's "pressed" for *katit* is less precise -- *katit* (from *katat*, "to beat, crush") denotes hand-crushing in a mortar, not mechanical pressing. |
| **Severity** | **Minor** (EMET vs. LOGOS: word order) / **Moderate** (PNEUMA: "pressed" vs. "beaten" -- different physical processes with different connotations). |

### v. 20 -- *ve-yiqkhu*: "they shall take" vs. "they bring" vs. "to bring"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and they shall take to you" |
| LOGOS | "that they bring to you" |
| PNEUMA | "to bring you" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Follows LOGOS** -- "that they bring" renders *ve-yiqkhu* (qal imperfect of *laqakh*, "to take") with the purposive sense carried by the *ve-* consecutive following the command verb. EMET's "and they shall take" is more literal but "take to you" is somewhat awkward in English; "bring" conveys the directional sense of *eleikha* ("to you") more naturally. |
| **Severity** | **Minor** -- synonym choice with slight nuance difference. |

### v. 21 -- *ya'arokh*: "arrange" vs. "keep burning" / "tend"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "shall arrange" |
| LOGOS | "shall arrange" |
| PNEUMA | "are to keep it burning" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Follows EMET/LOGOS** -- *ya'arokh* (qal imperfect of *arakh*, "to arrange, set in order") is a priestly technical term for tending the lamp. PNEUMA's "keep burning" interprets the result rather than translating the action. |
| **Severity** | **Moderate** -- PNEUMA replaces a specific Hebrew verb with an interpretive paraphrase that loses the priestly-technical language. |

### v. 21 -- *khuqqat olam*: "statute forever" vs. "statute of perpetuity" vs. "lasting ordinance"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "a statute of perpetuity" |
| LOGOS | "a statute forever" |
| PNEUMA | "a lasting ordinance" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Follows LOGOS** -- "a statute forever" is the most natural English rendering of the construct chain *khuqqat olam*. EMET's "of perpetuity" is unnecessarily formal. PNEUMA's "lasting ordinance" weakens *khuqqah* ("statute, inscribed decree") to the milder "ordinance" and reduces *olam* ("forever, age") to "lasting." |
| **Severity** | **Minor** (EMET) / **Moderate** (PNEUMA -- both key terms are rendered with weaker English equivalents). |

### v. 21 -- *me-et benei Yisrael*: clause placement
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "for their generations from the Sons of Israel" |
| LOGOS | "for their generations from the Sons of Israel" |
| PNEUMA | "for all generations among the Sons of Israel" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Follows EMET/LOGOS** -- "from the Sons of Israel" preserves *me-et* ("from") -- the oil is supplied *from* the people. PNEUMA's "among the Sons of Israel" changes the preposition, shifting the sense from sourcing (the community provides the oil) to location (the law applies among the community). |
| **Severity** | **Minor** -- subtle but real change in the directional sense of *me-et*. |

---

## Section-Level Disagreements

### Section Headings
| Translator | vv. 1-8 | vv. 9-19 | vv. 20-21 |
|---|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The Bronze Altar" | "The Courtyard of the Tabernacle" | "The Oil for the Lamp" |
| LOGOS | "The Altar of Burnt Offering" | "The Courtyard of the Tabernacle" | "Oil for the Lamp" |
| PNEUMA | "The Bronze Altar -- Where Sacrifice Meets Heaven" | "The Courtyard -- Defining Sacred Space" | "The Perpetual Lamp -- Light That Never Goes Out" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"The Bronze Altar"** | **"The Courtyard of the Tabernacle"** | **"The Oil for the Lamp"** |

**Note:** LOGOS's "The Altar of Burnt Offering" adds *ha-olah* ("burnt offering"), which does not appear in this chapter (it appears at 30:28; 31:9; 35:16; 38:1; 40:6, 10, 29). EMET's "The Bronze Altar" is more accurate for this passage. PNEUMA's subtitles are evocative but editorialize beyond the text.

---

## Summary Statistics

| Category | Count |
|---|---|
| Total disagreements logged | 15 |
| Trivial (synonym/style only) | 3 |
| Minor (defensible alternatives) | 8 |
| Moderate (affects meaning or key terminology) | 4 |
| Severe (substantive misreading) | 0 |
| SOPHIA follows EMET | 4 |
| SOPHIA follows LOGOS | 5 |
| SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS together | 5 |
| SOPHIA follows PNEUMA | 0 |
| SOPHIA follows EMET/PNEUMA | 1 |
| All three agree | 9 of 21 verses (no disagreement logged) |

---

*Disagreement log compiled by SOPHIA. All severity ratings reflect translational judgment, not translator quality.*
